title: Acts Constituting Illegal Recruitment
content:
The following acts constitute illegal recruitment under the 2002 POEA Rules on Overseas Employment of OFWs:

1. Charging or accepting any amount greater than that specified in the schedule of allowable fees prescribed by the Secretary, or making a worker pay any amount greater than that actually loaned or advanced to him.
2. Furnishing or publishing any false notice, information, or document related to recruitment or employment.
3. Giving any false notice, testimony, information, or document, or committing any act of misrepresentation to secure a license or authority under the Labor Code.
4. Inducing or attempting to induce a worker already employed to quit employment to offer another, unless to liberate the worker from oppressive terms.
5. Influencing or attempting to influence any person or entity not to employ any worker who has not applied through the agency.
6. Engaging in recruitment or placement of workers in jobs harmful to public health, morality, or dignity of the Republic of the Philippines as prohibited by law.
7. Obstructing or attempting to obstruct inspection by the Secretary or authorized representative.
8. Failing to submit required reports on employment status, placement, vacancies, remittance of foreign exchange earnings, separations, departures, and other information as required by the Secretary.
9. Substituting or altering to the prejudice of the worker, employment contracts approved and verified by DOLE without DOLE approval.
10. For officers or agents of recruitment agencies to become officers or members of travel agency boards or be involved in travel agency management.
11. Withholding or denying travel documents from applicant workers before departure for unauthorized monetary or financial consideration.
12. Failing to actually deploy workers without valid reason as determined by DOLE.
13. Failing to reimburse expenses incurred by the worker for documentation and processing when deployment does not take place without the worker's fault.
